questions.question: A parent has <app-card [title]="pageTitle" />. What does [title] mean here?
options: It sets the HTML title attribute on the element
It binds the parent's pageTitle expression to the child's @Input() title property
It creates a two-way binding between parent and child
It passes a string literal 'pageTitle' to the child

questions.question: When should you use @Output() and EventEmitter instead of a shared service?
options: Never — shared services are always better
When the communication is between siblings at the same nesting level
When a direct child needs to notify its immediate parent of a user action
When you need to broadcast an event to all components in the app

questions.question: What is the key difference between @Input() product and input.required<Product>() in Angular 17.1+?
options: There is no difference — they compile to the same code
input() returns a Signal; reading the value requires calling it as a function: product()
@Input() works with standalone components; input() only works with NgModules
input() is slower because it uses change detection

questions.question: You emit this.product from an @Output EventEmitter. The parent then mutates the received object. What problem can this cause?
options: The child's template will not update because Angular blocks external mutations
It throws a runtime error because EventEmitter payloads are frozen
The parent and child share the same object reference, so the child's state is silently mutated
Angular automatically creates a deep copy, so this is safe

questions.question: How do siblings (two components with the same parent) best communicate?
options: Sibling A emits an @Output; the parent catches it and passes it down via @Input to sibling B
Sibling A directly accesses sibling B's public properties
Both siblings use document.dispatchEvent() to broadcast native DOM events
Only one sibling can hold state; the other must always be stateless
